exemplar.title: To what extent does General Motorsâ shift towards electric vehicle production in 2023 affect its profitability?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent does General Motorsâ shift towards electric vehicle production in 2023 affect its profitability?

subject.title: Business Management
subject.description:
**IB Business Management** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **business fundamentals, human resource management, finance and accounts, marketing, and operations management**, with a strong focus on **case study analysis, real-world business applications, and strategic decision-making**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| **Assessment** | **Format** | **SL** | **HL** | **Weighting** |
|----------------|------------|--------|--------|---------------|
| **Paper 1** | Case study-based exam | 1h 30min | 1h 30min | SL: 35%, HL: 25% |
| **Paper 2** | Core themes exam | 1h 30min | 1h 30min | SL: 35%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 3** | Social enterprise case study | N/A | 1h 15min | HL: 25% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Written commentary (SL) / Research project (HL) | Various | Various | SL: 30%, HL: 20% |

strengths: Clear identification of âchangeâ as the key concept from the outset
Multiple references to policy shifts and EV transition of GM
Links concept to profitability analysis in introduction and conclusion
weaknesses: Concept not consistently woven into analytical sections (BCG and Porter focus more on product/strategy than on change)
Introduction briefly mentions âsustainabilityâ instead of the chosen concept
Analysis sections lack explicit commentary tying margin shifts directly to organizational change
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although the student shows good understanding of change and applies it clearly in parts of the report, the concept is only partially integrated into every analytical tool. Occasional lapses in terminology and missing explicit links in mid-sections place performance in band 3 out of 5.

strengths: Four supporting documents provided, meeting the quantitative requirement
Documents (financials, sales data, CEO interview, report extract) directly inform analysis
Provide both numerical and qualitative insight
weaknesses: Formatting errors in sales figures (e.g., â+1.045.83%â) reduce clarity
Lengthy transcript in the Forbes interview dilutes focus
Absence of a concise ratio summary table to enhance depth
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student supplies a sufficient number of relevant documents but several typographical and formatting flaws, plus lack of concise summarization, limit depth. This aligns with band 2 (generally relevant but lacking full depth) out of 4.

strengths: Selection of profitability ratios, BCG matrix and Porterâs strategies demonstrates awareness of key frameworks
Correct basic application of each tool to GMâs data
weaknesses: Table headings and units are missing or unclear
Superficial thresholds in BCG categorization not defined
Link between Porter framework and research question remains descriptive rather than analytical
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the student chooses appropriate tools, application is only adequate and occasionally descriptive. The relevance to the research question is not always sharply drawn. This performance fits band 2 (some tools selected with superficial application) out of 4.
feedback.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

strengths: Accurate extraction of sales and financial data for ratio calculations
Insightful explanation linking margin movements to battery-material costs and cost controls
Use of BCG and Porter analyses to evaluate product positions
weaknesses: Gross margin formula incorrectly presented (missing division by revenue)
Data entry errors and inconsistent units undermine precision
Lack of broader industry benchmarks and deeper integration across tools
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student demonstrates adequate selection and use of data with some effective analysis, but formula mistakes, data errors and inconsistent unit handling prevent sustained, thorough evaluation. This corresponds to band 3 out of 5.

strengths: Conclusions are fully consistent with presented evidence
Explicitly answers the research question regarding profitability impact
weaknesses: Does not compare findings against an initial hypothesis or expectations
Summary remains descriptive without deeper interpretive insight
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Conclusions draw appropriately on analysis and answer the research question, but lack critical reflection or comparison to forecasted outcomes. This matches band 2 (some conclusions consistent but limited depth) out of 3.

strengths: Clear table of contents and logical separation of sections
Research question and major headings are easy to locate
weaknesses: Transitions between tools and conclusion are abrupt
Introductory roadmap could better signal which metrics will be used
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The report has an identifiable structure, but limited cohesion in transitions and guidance places it at band 1 (limited structure) out of 2.

strengths: All required elements (word count, code, declaration, references, documents) are present
Figures and tables are generally legible
weaknesses: Multiple typographical and formatting errors (inline math, footnotes, crowded BCG grid)
Inconsistent citation and URL formatting detract from professionalism
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although all elements are included, pervasive formatting and presentation flaws impair readability. This performance aligns with band 1 (one or more required elements poorly executed) out of 2.

strandSelections.reasoning: The IA follows a logical structure with a clear table of contents, numbered sections, and separation of analysis, conclusion and documents.  This meets the descriptor for an **appropriate structure throughout** (band 2).

The documents are directly relevant (official financials and sales data, strategic commentary from the CEO) and give both numeric depth and qualitative insight, supplying a **range of ideas** used in the analysis.  Hence the top relevance/depth band is justified.
Word count, personal code, declaration, references, supporting documents and images are all present.  Hence all required elements are included, satisfying the highest presentation band.
Four supporting documents are provided (Q4 sales table, income statement, CEO interview, Annual Report extract).  This sits within the 3-5 range and therefore meets the highest quantitative band.

Tools are applied correctly (ratio calculations, BCG categorisation, discussion of cost leadership & differentiation).  Yet the linkage to the research question is sometimes descriptive; e.g., Porterâs grid lists points without quantifying profitability impact.  Application is **adequate but not consistently sharp**, matching band 3.

Financial statements and model-level sales figures are accurately extracted and used in ratio calculations and the BCG table, indicating **sufficient selection and competent use** of primary numerical data.  While broader industry benchmarks are absent, the evidence surpasses âadequateâ, meriting band 4.

The narrative links declining gross margin to battery-material costs, connects profit-margin improvement to cost controls, and evaluates product positions via BCG.  Integration across tools is generally effective, though not fully sustained.  This aligns with **âmostly effective analysis with some integrationâ**âband 4.
The research question (âto what extentâ¦affect profitabilityâ) is answered explicitly: impact is described as âmoderateâ with supporting rationale.  Therefore band 3 is appropriate.

The student selects three appropriate tools (profitability ratios, BCG Matrix, Porterâs Generic Strategies).  These cover finance, marketing and strategy, representing an **adequate** but not exhaustive toolkit.  No cash-flow forecasts or investment-appraisal techniques are applied, so effectiveness is solid rather than outstandingâband 3 fits best.
The conclusion synthesises ratio results, BCG insights and Porter analysis, and is **fully consistent** with the evidence presented.  No contradictory claims are made.

GM is consistently central: all data (financial statements, sales by model, CEO interview) and every tool (ratios, BCG, Porter) refer directly to the firm.  The analysis explains how EV production, costs, and product mix affect GMâs profitability, demonstrating **strong, partially integrated** connection throughout.  Minor lapsesâsuch as generic statements about EV industry trendsâprevent full integration, so band 4 is most appropriate.

The student explicitly selects CHANGE and links it to GMâs 2023 EV transition in the introduction, profitability discussion and conclusion.  References to âpolicy change,â âtransition,â and âflux periodâ demonstrate **clear understanding** of the concept and its strategic implications.  However, the idea of change is not woven consistently into every analytical section (e.g., the BCG matrix and Porter analysis are framed more around product portfolio and competitive advantage than around managing change itself).  This shows strong but only **partially integrated** use, aligning best with band 4.

annotations.comment: Excellent identification of the key concept âCHANGEâ at the outset ensures the assessment is grounded in the rubricâs required concept.

The equation block defines gross profit margin as âTotal Revenue â COGSâ without dividing by revenue. Correct the formula to$$
	ext{GPM} = \frac{\text{Revenue} - \text{COGS}}{\text{Revenue}} \times 100
$$.
The inline math shows âTotal Reviewâ instead of âTotal Revenue,â suggesting an OCR or typographical error. Update to the correct label.
The description of the gross profit margin definition is clear, but consider adding a brief link to how change in cost structure reflects the key concept of change.
In the sales table, the Q4 growth for GMC Hummer EV Pickup shows â+1.045.83%,â which is clearly a data entry error. Correct the percentage figure.
The research question is clearly stated, but consider specifying which profitability metrics (e.g., gross margin, ROE) will be used from the introduction to guide the reader.
The table of contents is comprehensive and logically structured, aiding navigation and demonstrating effective organization.
The introduction opens with âsustainability,â which deviates from the selected key concept of change. Align terminology consistently to strengthen concept integration.
Footnote formatting is inconsistent (e.g., â<sup>1</sup>â). Use a standard citation style to improve readability and compliance.
Units in the ratio calculation mix millions with decimal values (e.g., dividing 29,843 by 156.735). Ensure consistent units or clearly state the conversion to maintain accuracy.
The Profit Margin section correctly applies the ratio but does not explicitly tie variation to the concept of change. Add commentary connecting margin movements to strategic shifts.
The narrative references OnStar and Super Cruise streams without citation. Provide a source or footnote to substantiate this qualitative insight.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: Table 1 has no header labels for columns 2â4. Include clear headers and units to improve interpretability of sales data.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: The Porterâs Generic Strategies analysis outlines cost leadership and differentiation but lacks quantification of how each impacts profitability. Incorporate specific metrics.
BCG categorization lists models but lacks quantitative thresholds for âhighâ vs âlowâ market share or growth. Define criteria used for each category.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: The âDogsâ section notes no current entries but then mentions Bolt models for monitoring. Clarify if any models truly fall into this cell or leave blank if none.
The visual BCG grid on page 8 is crowded; consider reformatting the matrix with clear axis labels and consistent bullet markers.
The concluding text summarises the BCG but does not explicitly tie results back to the concept of change. Add a sentence linking portfolio shifts to organizational change management.
The conclusion summarises findings but does not compare results against any initial hypothesis or expectation. Adding that comparison would deepen analysis.
The framework description omits an in-text citation for Porter (1985). Include a reference to reinforce theoretical grounding.
The opening sentence on Porterâs page effectively transitions from BCG but could briefly remind the reader of how competitive strategies reflect organizational change.
The balance sheet table in Supporting Document 2 is rich in detail but lacks narrative interpretation. Add bullet points to highlight key changes affecting profitability.
The quote from Barra is repeated twice with similar wording. Consider paraphrasing one instance to improve concision and focus.
Sales percentages in Supporting Document 1 include formatting errors (e.g., â+1.045.83%â). Verify all growth figures and correct typographical mistakes.
Supporting Document 2 provides extensive financial statements; however, including a brief ratio table (e.g., liquidity ratios) would enhance analysis relevance.
One reference URL is split across two lines and disrupts readability. Combine it into a single line or hyperlink format.
The Forbes interview transcript is lengthy. Condense to the most relevant quotes that support your analysis to maintain focus and brevity.
Supporting Document 4 includes forward-looking statements and risk factors. Consider trimming this background to focus on data directly tied to your profitability analysis.
Consider adding a brief summary at each transition (e.g., before the Porter section) to remind readers how each tool addresses the research question.
The transition from analysis to conclusion could be smoother; add a linking sentence that revisits the research question before summarizing findings.
